summary: The app writes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl\ShowDisconnectedDevices = 1 to expose disconnected audio devices in the classic Sound control panel. Procmon captures on 2026-03-14 confirmed that rundll32.exe launching mmsys.cpl queries this exact value and reads both Data:1 and Data:0 when the value is toggled. A Ghidra headless pass on 2026-03-26 against mmsys.cpl also decompiled the handler that calls SHGetValueW and SHSetValueW for ShowDisconnectedDevices under the DeviceCpl branch. A focused v3.1 runtime lane on 2026-03-27 then re-ran the missing -> 1 -> missing cycle in Win25H2Clean, launched the classic Sound control panel, captured a WPR GeneralProfile placeholder trace, and ended with healthy shell state. That keeps the record aligned with the v3.1 contract without changing the underlying class basis: Procmon plus Ghidra remain the class-driving proof, and the runtime lane adds explicit rollback and VM-safe runner coverage.

setting.name: Show Disconnected Audio Devices
setting.casual_explanation: This makes disconnected audio devices appear in the classic Sound control panel. It is mainly useful for troubleshooting, advanced audio setup, or cleaning up stale devices.
setting.why_people_change_it: People enable it when they need to inspect endpoints that are currently unplugged or otherwise not active.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Showing disconnected devices can help debugging, but it can also expose stale or confusing entries that most users never need to see. This is a runtime-observed control-panel preference rather than a Microsoft-documented policy surface.
setting.professional_notes: The app writes ShowDisconnectedDevices = 1 under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl. On 2026-03-14, Procmon captures of rundll32.exe launching shell32.dll,Control_RunDLL mmsys.cpl,,0 showed RegQueryValue reads for ShowDisconnectedDevices with Data:1 and Data:0 when the value was toggled and restored. On 2026-03-26, a Ghidra headless pass against mmsys.cpl decompiled the DeviceCpl flag handlers and showed SHGetValueW and SHSetValueW calls for ShowDisconnectedDevices and the same DeviceCpl branch. On 2026-03-27, the mapped v3.1 runtime runner re-ran the missing -> 1 -> missing cycle in Win25H2Clean, launched the classic Sound control panel, captured a WPR GeneralProfile placeholder trace, and preserved healthy shell state. That tightens the reversible-cycle and VM-safe-runner coverage, but the class-driving consumer proof still comes from the Procmon reads and the code-side proof still comes from the Ghidra pass. No primary Microsoft Learn or ADMX source for the DeviceCpl contract was captured in this pass, so the record remains a runtime-observed preference rather than a fully documented Microsoft contract.

allowed_values.label: Hide disconnected audio devices
allowed_values.meaning: Procmon confirmed that the classic Sound control panel reads ShowDisconnectedDevices = 0 on this build. This is the observed off state for the preference.

allowed_values.label: Show disconnected audio devices
allowed_values.meaning: Procmon confirmed that the classic Sound control panel reads ShowDisconnectedDevices = 1 on this build. This is the observed show-disconnected state used by the app.

targets.notes: Validated as a runtime-observed control-panel preference. The absent-value default was not captured in this pass.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes ShowDisconnectedDevices = 1, and Procmon confirmed that the classic Sound control panel reads the same value as a live preference on this build.

windows_defaults.label: Default state not captured
windows_defaults.applies_to: Classic Sound control panel profiles where the value is absent

states.rationale: This pass validated the 0 and 1 states through Procmon, but it did not capture the absent-value default.

windows_defaults.notes: Use this record as a validated runtime preference map for 0 and 1, not as a complete Microsoft-documented default model. The 2026-03-27 v3.1 runtime lane preserved a missing-value baseline and restored it cleanly, but it did not capture how the Sound control panel interprets the missing state.

recommended_profiles.label: Show disconnected audio devices
avoid_for: General users who do not need extra endpoint visibility

states.rationale: Set the runtime-observed preference to the show-disconnected state that the Sound control panel reads on this build.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You need the classic Sound control panel to expose disconnected devices.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the cleanest possible device list with fewer stale entries.
tradeoffs: Disconnected devices become visible in the classic Sound control panel.
The endpoint list can become noisier or more confusing.

evidence.title: Procmon capture - Sound control panel ShowDisconnectedDevices runtime reads
evidence.summary: Procmon captured rundll32.exe launching shell32.dll,Control_RunDLL mmsys.cpl,,0 and querying HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl\ShowDisconnectedDevices with Data:1 and Data:0 in separate reversible captures. That proves the classic Sound control panel consumes this registry value on this build.

evidence.title: Our Ghidra decompilation - mmsys.cpl DeviceCpl flag handlers
evidence.summary: A headless Ghidra pass on mmsys.cpl decompiled the functions that call SHGetValueW and SHSetValueW for HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl\ShowHiddenDevices and ShowDisconnectedDevices. That gives direct code-side proof that the classic Sound control panel reads and writes these DeviceCpl flags.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean v3.1 runtime lane - Audio DeviceCpl ShowDisconnectedDevices
evidence.summary: A v3.1 runtime runner on Win25H2Clean started from a missing-value baseline, wrote ShowDisconnectedDevices = 1, launched the classic Sound control panel via rundll32.exe, captured a WPR GeneralProfile placeholder trace, restored the missing baseline, and ended with healthy shell state. This strengthens the reversible-cycle and VM-safe-runner coverage, but it does not replace the Procmon consumer proof.

decision.why: The registry path is validated as a live runtime preference because the classic Sound control panel queried ShowDisconnectedDevices with both 1 and 0 in reversible Procmon captures on this build, Ghidra decompiled the mmsys.cpl handlers that read and write the same DeviceCpl flag, and the app writes the same show-disconnected state with a clean restore story. The 2026-03-27 v3.1 runtime lane adds an explicit missing -> 1 -> missing VM cycle with a control-panel launch, WPR placeholder trace, and healthy shell recovery, but the class basis still rests on the Procmon plus Ghidra convergence rather than on the runtime lane alone.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: Procmon: audio-devicecpl-query-20260314-pml.md and audio-devicecpl-query-zero-20260314-pml.md captured rundll32.exe reading ShowDisconnectedDevices with Data:1 and Data:0. Ghidra: audio-devicecpl-ghidra.md decompiled the mmsys.cpl SHGetValueW/SHSetValueW handlers for ShowDisconnectedDevices. Runtime lane: audio-devicecpl-runtime-showdisconnecteddevices-20260327-104736/summary.json preserved missing -> 1 -> missing, launched the control panel, and ended with healthy shell state.
validation_proof.notes: The record-specific v3.1 re-audit note consolidates the existing Procmon and Ghidra proof with the 2026-03-27 runtime-runner summary. The class-driving proof remains the Procmon plus Ghidra convergence; the runtime lane is recorded as supplemental rollback and VM-safety evidence.
